moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Van Geet Opening, an offbeat but principled approach where we develop our knight before the central pawns. This move controls d5 and e4 immediately, signaling we're not playing a standard 1.e4 or 1.d4 but rather pursuing our own setup. From c3, the knight supports a future e4 advance and prepares to challenge Black's center naturally.

Black stakes a claim in the center, occupying e5 and preparing to develop freely. This is a natural response to our offbeat setup—Black doesn't have to worry about our knight controlling e4, so they occupy it immediately. We're now facing a more complex position than mainstream openings, which is exactly why the Van Geet attracts creative players.

We support our d4 advance (if we choose it) and prepare f4, the key tactical shot of the Liebig Gambit. By playing e3 instead of developing normally, we're building toward a pawn break that will disrupt Black's center and create tactical chances. This move is slightly slow but purposeful—we're committing to a gambit plan rather than conventional development.

Black presses in the center, mirroring our expansion and claiming space in the middle. With pawns on d5 and e5, Black has a strong central presence and prevents us from playing d4 easily. This ambitious response shows Black understands the position—occupying space before we can organize our own pieces is the principled defense.

This is the characteristic move of the Liebig Gambit, attacking along the h5-e2 diagonal and immediately threatening tactical ideas. Our queen eyes h7 and supports an aggressive f4 break. While it looks sharp and somewhat unusual, the idea is sound: we're creating immediate tactical pressure on Black's kingside, and the queen on h5 will support our center pawns when we push f4, while also keeping an eye on f7.

Black develops with purpose, attacking our e4 square (which we haven't occupied yet) and preparing to fianchetto or develop the bishop. The knight on f6 also defends h7 against our queen's threats and prepares to reroute to e4 or d6 depending on how the game develops. This is a solid, flexible move that doesn't commit Black to a specific plan yet.
